# THE PICK: Baker Hughes (BKR)

*Generated by `/stock-pick` on 2026-06-21. Research output, not financial advice — see disclaimer.*

- **Company:** Baker Hughes Company
- **Sector / Sub-Industry:** Energy / Oil & Gas Equipment & Services
- **Market cap:** ~$58B
- **Price:** $58.41 · analyst mean target $71.24 (+22%) · consensus "buy"
- **Panel vote:** BKR 4 pts (top pick of 2 of 4 agents), NVDA 2, GE 2

## Thesis — "the next MU" in one paragraph

Baker Hughes is the cleanest mispriced expression of the AI data-center *power*
shortage. The market files BKR under "oil stock" — half the company is cyclical
oilfield services, it screens cheap (~18x trailing earnings, the lowest margins
and composite score of our high-shortage names), and it's only the **#3** maker
of large gas turbines. That stigma is masking a hard structural change: its
Industrial & Energy Technology (IET) turbine business is **sold out through
2028**, carrying a **record $33.1B backlog** at a **1.5× book-to-bill**, and its
brand-new data-center turbine line scaled from **$0 to ~$1B in a single year**.
The three large-frame turbine leaders (GE Vernova, Siemens Energy, Mitsubishi)
are 4–5 years backlogged, so overflow demand for faster-delivery power equipment
is pouring into BKR's NovaLT and aeroderivative units. This is the same
"order-book booked years out" signal that flagged Micron's HBM call — except
here the backlog is contracted, physical, take-or-pay-grade rotating equipment,
and the market hasn't re-rated the stock for it yet.

## The shortage + backlog evidence (sourced, dated)

- **NovaLT gas turbines sold out through 2028** — the single hardest "capacity
  sold out" statement in the entire 12-name research set (Q1/Q2 2026 calls).
- **Record IET backlog $33.1B** (Q2 2026), IET orders $4.9B in the quarter at a
  **1.5× book-to-bill** — bookings far outrunning shipments.
- **Data-center turbine revenue $0 (2024) → ~$1B (2025)**, with new orders (e.g.
  Feb 2026 Twenty20 Energy: 10 Frame-5 units ~250 MW; 16-turbine/270 MW order).
- LNG cycle still robust (Rio Grande Train 5; 64-unit Petrobras LM2500/LM6000
  frame agreement), providing a second contracted demand stream.
- 3rd straight quarterly beat: Q2 2026 EPS $0.58 vs $0.49 est, revenue $6.59B.

## Why it qualifies as a leader (and the one caveat)

BKR is the deterministic screen's **category leader in Oil & Gas Equipment &
Services** (the #1 S&P 500 name by market cap in that GICS sub-industry) and is
**#1 in LNG turbomachinery**. The honest caveat: in *large* gas turbines
specifically it is **#3**, capturing spillover rather than leading. The bull
case partly rests on that being a feature — BKR delivers faster precisely
because it isn't backlogged 5 years like the leaders.

## Return scenario (12–18 months)

- **Base case ≈ +25–35%:** IET backlog converts, data-center turbine revenue
  scales off a low base, modest multiple lift. Aligns with the +22% analyst
  mean target as a floor-ish case.
- **Bull case ≈ +60–80%:** the market explicitly **re-segments BKR from "oil
  services" to "AI/power infrastructure,"** the data-center turbine ramp beats
  the $1B marker, and the multiple expands from a depressed oilfield base toward
  an electrical-equipment multiple.
- **Asymmetry:** the contracted, sold-out backlog provides a downside floor even
  if oilfield services weakens — which is what makes this a risk/reward bet, not
  just a momentum chase.

## Key risks / what would invalidate the thesis

- **Sticky (and partly fair) stigma:** a cyclical oilfield-services downturn
  (oil-capex cuts) drags reported earnings and keeps the "oil multiple"
  anchored, delaying the IET re-rating regardless of the sold-out turbine book.
- **Data-center power demand durability:** the turbine pipeline is the linchpin;
  an AI-capex pause or grid-interconnection delays push booked backlog out
  rather than converting it.
- **Not the turbine #1:** if the large-frame leaders add capacity faster than
  expected, BKR's spillover advantage narrows.
- **Low organic growth today** (screen revenue growth only +2.5%) — the thesis
  is about the *forward* IET mix shift, not the current blended top line.

## How the panel adjudicated

Two independent lenses converged on BKR for different reasons, which is the
strongest signal a 4-agent panel can give:
- **Agent A (supply-chain, conv. 8):** BKR top / CAT runner-up — "sold out
  through 2028" is the hardest multi-year visibility in the set, and on a
  ~$58B cap each incremental booked gigawatt actually re-rates the equity
  (vs. getting lost in GE/NVDA's trillion-dollar bases).
- **Agent D (contrarian, conv. 8):** BKR top / NEM runner-up — textbook
  stigma-masked asymmetry: a contracted power-infrastructure backlog hiding
  inside an "oil" multiple the market refuses to re-rate.
- **Agent B (growth/momentum, conv. 8):** NVDA top / APH runner-up — picked the
  fastest *current* compounder; BKR's low headline growth scores poorly on this
  lens (a fair counterpoint: the BKR thesis is forward mix-shift, not today's
  growth rate).
- **Agent C (quality/moat, conv. 8):** GE top / LLY runner-up — wanted a true
  #1 with the most durable moat; correctly dinged BKR for being #3 in turbines.

**Adjudication:** Per the doctrine, strength + recency of the shortage/backlog
evidence breaks ties, and a thesis the market has already fully priced loses to
one with room to surprise. BKR wins on **both** axes simultaneously — the
hardest sold-out signal *and* the least-priced-in setup — whereas the strong
alternatives (NVDA, GE) were explicitly flagged by multiple agents as
consensus/priced-in. NVDA remains the higher-*quality* business and GE the more
durable moat; BKR is the better **explosive-return / asymmetry** bet, which is
the mandate. It also passes every gate of the deterministic screen, so the pick
does not override the doctrine.
